content.subtitle: Use
items: General or scientific facts and definitions: If you **heat** water to 100°C, it **boils** and turns into steam.

content.subtitle: Form
content.formula: if + a present tense, will + bare infinitive

content.subtitle: Use
items: Real or likely conditions in the present or future and their results in the present and future: If you **post** that photo online, your friends **will see** it straight away!
: If you'**re studying** all evening, we'**ll have** dinner a little later.
: If you **have charged** your phone, you **won't have** any battery problems on the way there.

content.subtitle: Be careful
items: We can also use may, might, can, could, shall, should, ought to, have to instead of will, depending on the meaning. ✓ If you **post** that photo online, you **might get** lots of comments!

We can also use should + bare infinitive instead of present simple. This suggests the situation is possible, but unlikely to happen. ✓ If you **should come** across any suspicious messages, you'll want to report them immediately.

We can also use a form of the first conditional to give instructions about real or likely situations in the present or future. This is: if + a present tense, imperative. ✓ If you **decide** to create a social media account, **show** me!

sections.title: Unless, in case, as/so long as, provided (that)

content.subtitle: Meanings and examples
rows: I'll reply to your message **unless** my phone dies. (= except if my phone dies / if my phone doesn't die)
Save the document **in case** the internet goes down. (= because it might go down)
**As long as** you share the password, we can all use the account. / **So long as** you share the password, we can all use the account.
**Provided (that)** you share the password, we can all use the account.

content.subtitle: Form
content.formula: if + past simple or past continuous, would + bare infinitive

content.subtitle: Use
items: Impossible, unlikely or hypothetical conditions in the present or future and their results in the present or future: If you **had** a faster internet connection, you **would download** films in seconds! / If you **were designing** your own app, **would** you **make** it free?
Advice: If I **were** you, I would think very carefully about sharing personal data online. (more formal) / If I **was** you, I'd delete that comment right away! (more informal)

content.subtitle: Be careful
items: We can also use *might* or *could* instead of *would*, depending on the meaning. (Note: *could* here often means *would be able to*.) ✓ If we **were** better at coding, we **could build** our own website.
We can also use *could* in the *if* clause. Here, it means *was/were able to*. ✓ If I **could** code, I'd create my own app.

content.subtitle: Form
content.formula: if + past perfect (simple or continuous), would + have + past participle

content.subtitle: Use
items: Hypothetical conditions in the past and their results in the past: If you **had used** a strong password, no one **would have hacked** your account! (= You didn't use a strong password so people hacked it.) / If she'**d been checking** her messages, she **would have noticed** the warning earlier. (= She wasn't checking her messages so she didn't notice the warning.)

content.subtitle: Be careful
items: We can also use *might*, *could* or *should* instead of *would*, depending on the meaning. ✓ If I **had backed up** my files, I **might / could / should** have recovered the data.

content.subtitle: Form 1
content.formula: if + past perfect (simple or continuous), would + bare infinitive

content.subtitle: Use
items: Hypothetical past condition and a present result: If I **had read** the terms and conditions, I **wouldn't be** having these privacy problems now. (= I didn't read the terms and conditions so I'm having privacy problems now.)

content.subtitle: Form 2
content.formula: if + past simple or past continuous, would + have + past participle

content.subtitle: Use
items: Hypothetical present condition and a past result: If I **had** a laptop, I **would have joined** the video call last night. (= I don't have a laptop so I didn't join the call last night.)

content.subtitle: Form
items: Should I/you/he/etc … instead of If I/you/he/etc should …
Were I/you/he/etc … instead of If I/you/he/etc were …
Had I/you/he/etc … instead of If I/you/he/etc had …

content.subtitle: Use
items: More formal form of the first conditional (with should): **Should** the server go down, the IT team is prepared to act immediately. (= If the server should go down…)
More formal form of the second conditional: **Were** the app to crash, the developers would release a fix within hours. (= If the app were to crash…)
More formal form of the third conditional: **Had** the server gone down sooner, the IT team would have responded immediately. (= If the server had gone down sooner…)

content.subtitle: Be careful
items: For all conditional sentences (first, second, third, mixed, inverted), when the condition comes before the result it is usually followed by a comma. When the result comes first, no comma is necessary. ✓ If I had a laptop, I would have joined the call last night. ✓ I would have joined the call last night if I had a laptop.
In conditional sentences, modals (will, would, could, etc) are sometimes followed by a continuous infinitive. ✓ We'd still **be loading** if you hadn't restarted the router.

content.subtitle: American English note
content.description: With second and third conditionals in informal conversation, speakers of American English sometimes use would or would have in the if clause. This is very unusual in British English.
examples: US: What would you do if the app **crashed / would crash** right now? UK: What would you do if the app **crashed** right now?
US: I would have been furious if my account **had been hacked / would have been hacked**. UK: I would have been furious if my account **had been hacked**.

sections.title: A: Understanding conditionals
sections.instructions: Read each conditional sentence. Then answer the question by choosing YES or NO.

items.context: If my phone runs out of battery, I'll miss the group call.
items.sentence: Is it possible that my phone will run out of battery?

items.context: If my classmate became a famous YouTuber, she'd give everyone a free subscription.
items.sentence: Is it likely that my classmate will become a famous YouTuber?

items.context: If my classmate became a famous YouTuber, she'd give everyone a free subscription.
items.sentence: Is it possible that my classmate will become a famous YouTuber?

items.context: If Sarah weren't online right now, I wouldn't be able to finish the project.
items.sentence: Is Sarah online right now?

items.context: If Leo had had a good enough laptop, he'd have joined the video call.
items.sentence: Did Leo have a good enough laptop?

items.context: If Leo had had a good enough laptop, he'd have joined the video call.
items.sentence: Did Leo join the video call?

items.context: If Clara hadn't downloaded the app, she wouldn't have found out about the event.
items.sentence: Did Clara download the app?

items.context: If Clara hadn't downloaded the app, she wouldn't have found out about the event.
items.sentence: Did she find out about the event?

items.context: If the tech team had tested the software properly, they wouldn't have released the faulty update.
items.sentence: Did the tech team test the software properly?

items.context: If the tech team had tested the software properly, they wouldn't have released the faulty update.
items.sentence: Did they release the faulty update?

items.context: Had he not learned to code, he might not have launched his startup.
items.sentence: Did he learn to code?

items.context: Had he not learned to code, he might not have launched his startup.
items.sentence: Did he launch his startup?

sections.title: B: Complete the definitions
sections.instructions: Match each sentence beginning (1–8) with the correct ending (A–H).

items.sentence: If someone is dishonest, **C** you don't tell the truth.
items.answer: C – you don't tell the truth.

items.sentence: If someone avoids putting in any effort, **F** you don't work hard.
If you intimidate someone weaker than you, **A** you don't treat them with respect.
items.answer: A – you don't treat them with respect.

items.sentence: If you think you are superior to those around you, **H** you don't respect them.
If someone is very wealthy, **D** you have lots of money.
items.answer: D – you have lots of money.

items.sentence: If someone is your role model, **G** you respect them.
If you blame a person for a wrongdoing, **B** you say they have done something wrong.
items.answer: B – you say they have done something wrong.

items.sentence: If fortune is on your side, **E** good things happen to you.
items.answer: E – good things happen to you.

sections.title: C: First conditional
sections.instructions: Complete using the correct form of the verbs in the box.

items.sentence: If he ...... home before noon, he'll arrive at the venue in time.
They ...... me if the delivery arrives at the wrong address.
You shouldn't stay up so late if you ...... your driving test tomorrow morning.
If you ...... at the gym all morning, you must be exhausted by now.
If you should ...... my keys anywhere in the flat, let me know straight away!
We can choose a different film if you ...... that one at the cinema already.
If Emma ...... on that report for so long, she'll be glad to have the weekend free.
If you're going to the supermarket, ...... some milk while you're there!
If you speak to Tom before Friday, ...... to pass on my thanks!
You don't have to stay for dinner if you ...... to.

sections.title: D: Unless, in case, as long as, provided
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.

items.sentence: I'll finish the project on time **unless / if** something unexpected comes up.
You should save your work regularly **unless / in case** the computer crashes.
You can use my laptop **as long as / in case** you promise to look after it.
Let's try that new café **unless / if** you haven't been there before.
**Unless / So long as** I have my phone with me, I don't mind being away from home.
Bring a portable charger with you today **if / in case** your battery runs out.
You can post whatever you like **provided / unless** you don't share any personal information.
I'm not going to panic **unless / as long as** she hasn't called by midnight.

sections.title: E: Second conditional
sections.instructions: Write the complete second conditional sentence.

items.context: I share a flat with two friends, so I never feel lonely.
items.answer: If I lived alone, I'd get lonely.

items.context: Don has got so many assignments that he won't be going to the match tonight.
items.answer: If Don didn't have so much homework, he'd play football tonight.

items.context: I don't know how to swim, so I won't be joining the diving trip with Terry.
items.answer: If I could swim, I'd go scuba diving with Terry.

items.context: We can't get a pizza tonight because none of us has any money left.
items.answer: We'd order pizza if we had enough money.

items.context: We've been booked into different hotels, which means we won't be able to share a room.
items.answer: If we were staying in the same hotel, we could share a room.

items.context: You're the one in this situation, but I really think you should call Antony this minute!
items.answer: If I were you, I'd call Antony right now.

items.context: I've promised to help my dad fix something, so I won't be able to make it.
items.answer: I'd come if I didn't have to help my dad with something.

items.context: I go to bed much earlier than you, which is why I can get up early every day.
items.answer: If I went to bed as late as you, I wouldn't be able to get up early in the morning.

sections.title: F: Third conditional
sections.instructions: Match each sentence beginning (1–9) with the correct ending (A–I).

items.sentence: If my grandparents hadn't met at that dance hall in 1965, **D** I wouldn't have been born.
items.answer: D – I wouldn't have been born.

items.sentence: Beyoncé wouldn't have had such a successful career **G** if she hadn't been discovered.
items.answer: G – if she hadn't been discovered.

items.sentence: If the asteroid hadn't collided with the Earth millions of years ago, **B** the dinosaurs wouldn't have become extinct.
items.answer: B – the dinosaurs wouldn't have become extinct.

items.sentence: All the passengers on the Titanic might have survived **E** if there had been enough lifeboats.
items.answer: E – if there had been enough lifeboats.

items.sentence: If Spain hadn't won the World Cup in 2010, **I** I would have been very upset.
items.answer: I – I would have been very upset.

items.sentence: If I'd started studying French two years ago, **A** I would have done this book last year!
items.answer: A – I would have done this book last year!

items.sentence: If the financial crisis of 2008 hadn't occurred, **H** lots of things would have been different.
items.answer: H – lots of things would have been different.

items.sentence: If my neighbour had won the lottery last month, **C** he/she would have moved to a bigger house.
items.answer: C – he/she would have moved to a bigger house.

items.sentence: El Greco wouldn't have passed away in obscurity **F** if he had been recognised as a great painter during his lifetime.
items.answer: F – if he had been recognised as a great painter during his lifetime.

sections.title: G: Multiple choice (Conditionals)
sections.passage: If remote work (1) ...... as popular as it is today, it (2) ...... that technology has completely changed the way we live. But if that (3) ...... not true a decade ago – if most people were still commuting to offices every day – (4) ...... very little about working from home. And if I (5) ...... the advantages of remote working back then, how could I have imagined my current lifestyle? If a colleague (6) ...... this position last year, they would have been able to work from anywhere in the world. Whatever choices we make, every decision (7) ...... consequences for the future. For example, if you (8) ...... make the most of today's technology, you might find yourself falling behind. If you just (9) ...... positively about all the opportunities available to you, I'm sure (10) ...... absolutely fine!

sections.title: H: Mixed conditionals
sections.instructions: Complete using the correct form of the verbs in brackets.

items.sentence: If (you / do) ...... your research last night, you'd understand the topic much better today!
We (not / stand) ...... outside in the rain right now waiting for a taxi if (we / bring) ...... an umbrella with us.
If I (not / stay up) ...... until three in the morning watching videos, I (not / feel) ...... so exhausted right now!
items.answer: hadn't stayed up, wouldn't be feeling

items.sentence: (I / be) ...... in serious difficulty at the moment if you (not / help) ...... me with that presentation.
If I had a proper camera, (I / record) ...... that performance last night.
items.answer: I'd have recorded/I would have recorded

items.sentence: (I / ask) ...... for Jake's number when we met at the party if I (not / already / have) ...... a partner!
items.answer: I'd have asked, didn't already have

items.sentence: If Marcus (not / live) ...... so far from the city centre, he (not / be) ...... so late to the meeting yesterday.
Sophie (not / need) ...... extra coaching sessions last term if she (be) ...... as strong in science as her brother.
(you / get) ...... a scholarship to study abroad if the committee (not / offer) ...... you the grant?
items.answer: Would you have got, hadn't offered

items.sentence: If you (have) ...... as much experience as he does, (you / retire) ...... from the team by now?

sections.title: I: Inverted conditionals
sections.instructions: Complete the sentences so that the meaning remains the same.

items.sentence:
If her condition should improve, we will discharge her from hospital at once. **Should**
Should ......, we will discharge her from hospital at once.

Jade will have some fascinating stories to tell if she gets to interview a famous politician. **Should**
Should ......, she will have some fascinating stories to tell.
items.answer: Jade get to interview a famous politician

items.sentence:
If you were able to go abroad for the summer, which country would you choose? **Were**
Were ......, which country would you choose?
items.answer: you able to go abroad for the summer

items.sentence:
If I was to become a vet, I'd find working long hours on call very demanding. **Were**
Were ......, I'd find working long hours on call very demanding.

Our oceans would recover remarkably quickly if all environmental pollution stopped today. **Were**
Were ......, our oceans would recover remarkably quickly.
items.answer: all environmental pollution to stop today

items.sentence:
If the hole in the ozone layer had been discovered sooner, governments would have acted much earlier. **Had**
Had ......, governments would have acted much earlier.
items.answer: the hole in the ozone layer been discovered sooner

items.sentence:
I never would have pursued a career in education if I hadn't had such a good English teacher at school. **Had**
Had ......, I never would have pursued a career in education.
items.answer: I not had such a good English teacher at school

sections.title: J: Error correction (extra words)
sections.instructions: Read each line of the text. If there is an extra word, click it. If the line is correct, click OK.

items.sentence: Growing up with social media has many advantages. 'Wouldn't you feel much more ......
connected if you have had social media as a teenager?' some people say. ......
It's true that if I had [had] joined these platforms earlier, I would have built a larger ......
network by now, but I'm not sure that would have been entirely positive. ......
I think it's fine to use them provided for you don't spend too many hours online. ......
If my parents would had set stricter screen time rules, I might have developed different habits. ......
Then again, I do enjoy scrolling through my feed, except unless it becomes completely addictive. ......
If that happened, I'd have it to delete the app from my phone immediately. ......
There are moments when I wonder what my life would look like without any social media at all. ......
But I won't stop using it unless it will becomes a real problem – and even then, I'd probably miss it terribly! ......
